highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.145 Released
highlights.summary: The latest release adds `claude agents --json` to list live Claude sessions as JSON, making it easier to script session management with tools like tmux-resurrect or custom status bars. OTEL tracing improvements also land, with `agent_id` and `parent_agent_id` attributes on spans so background subagent traces now correctly nest under their parent. This is especially useful if you're building automation or monitoring pipelines around Claude Code sessions.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.144 Released
highlights.summary: Background sessions started via `claude --bg` can now be resumed with `/resume`, appearing alongside interactive sessions and clearly marked with a `bg` indicator. Completion notifications for background subagents now also show elapsed duration (e.g. '3h 2m 5s'), so you always know how long a long-running task took. This makes managing multiple parallel Claude Code sessions much more practical for complex workflows.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.142 Released
highlights.summary: The `claude agents` command gains a powerful set of new flags—`--model`, `--effort`, `--settings`, `--mcp-config`, and more—letting you fully configure dispatched background sessions from the command line. Fast mode now defaults to Opus 4.7, taking advantage of the newly released model for improved performance. If you regularly spin up background agents for large tasks, these flags give you fine-grained control over each session's behavior.

highlights.title: Introducing Claude Opus 4.7
highlights.summary: Anthropic has officially launched Claude Opus 4.7, the model now powering Fast mode in Claude Code v2.1.142 and beyond. This announcement covers the model's improved capabilities and what it means for coding workflows. If you rely on Claude Code for complex multi-step tasks, upgrading to a version that uses Opus 4.7 by default is a meaningful performance boost.

highlights.title: How Claude Code Works in Large Codebases
highlights.summary: This Anthropic blog post shares best practices for using Claude Code effectively in large, complex repositories—covering where to start, how to scope tasks, and how to avoid common pitfalls. It's directly applicable to developers who find Claude Code works great on small projects but struggle to scale it up. Bookmark this as a reference guide before tackling any substantial codebase refactor.

highlights.title: Mastering Claude Code in 30 minutes
highlights.summary: A focused 30-minute YouTube tutorial aimed at developers who want to move beyond the basics and get genuinely productive with Claude Code. The video covers practical workflows and key features you'll use day-to-day, making it a great watch during a lunch break. Even if you've used Claude Code before, revisiting fundamentals in a structured format often surfaces shortcuts you've missed.

highlights.title: FULL Claude Code Tutorial for Beginners in 2026! (Step-By-Step)
highlights.summary: A comprehensive step-by-step tutorial for complete beginners getting started with Claude Code in 2026, covering setup through practical usage. It's ideal if you're new to AI coding assistants and want a single resource to get up and running quickly without piecing together scattered documentation. Following along hands-on while watching will help you build muscle memory for the most common commands.

highlights.title: Session Amnesia: The Hidden Cost of Stateless AI Coding Assistants
highlights.summary: This article explores the productivity cost of AI coding assistants losing context between sessions, a problem that affects daily Claude Code users who work across multiple days or switch between tasks. Understanding this limitation helps you design better CLAUDE.md files and session strategies to preserve important context manually. It's a thoughtful read for anyone who has felt frustrated reexplaining project context to Claude Code at the start of each session.

tips_of_the_day: Use `claude agents --json` to pipe live session data into a jq filter or shell script—this lets you build custom tmux status bars or session pickers that always reflect what Claude Code is actually doing right now.

Before starting a long background agent task with `claude --bg`, use `--effort` and `--model` flags to tune the session for cost or speed—lower effort is often sufficient for routine refactors and can significantly reduce API usage.

Maintain a dedicated `CLAUDE.md` file at your project root with a 'Session Context' section you update at the end of each working day. Pasting a short summary of current goals and recent decisions gives Claude Code instant orientation at the start of your next session, fighting session amnesia without any extra tooling.
